content.text: Approved Model Catalog and Usage Policy

An approved model catalog is the enterprise governance mechanism that determines which AI models any team may use, under what conditions, and for which data classifications. Without a catalog, individual teams independently select and integrate models, creating an ungoverned sprawl of API keys, inconsistent data handling practices, and unreviewed vendor contracts. The catalog transforms ad-hoc AI adoption into a structured procurement and security process.

attrs.alt: Approved Model Catalog Governance Flow — from model request through security and legal review to API key issuance

content.text: A model catalog is a structured registry of AI models that have been reviewed and approved for use within the organization. Each entry records enough information to answer the questions that security, legal, and engineering teams will ask when a new project wants to use a model.
Where inference runs and data is retained
Highest sensitivity of data that may be sent
Explicit list of permitted workload types
Explicit list of disallowed workload types
Legal advice, medical diagnosis, HR decisions
Team or system account holding the key
Next scheduled catalog review for this entry

The catalog does not need to be elaborate. A well-maintained spreadsheet or a simple internal wiki page with these fields per entry is sufficient for most organizations under 1,000 employees. Larger organizations benefit from a dedicated internal developer portal or an IDP (Internal Developer Platform) that serves catalog entries via API to CI/CD pipelines.

The most operationally important field in a catalog entry is the maximum data classification. This field answers the question: what is the most sensitive data a developer may include in a prompt sent to this model? Getting this wrong in either direction creates risk — too restrictive and the catalog becomes an obstacle to legitimate use; too permissive and sensitive data is sent to a vendor whose contract and technical controls do not cover that data class.
Information already publicly available or intended for public release
None beyond standard API key management
Non-sensitive business information; disclosure causes limited harm
DPA in place; no logging of prompts by vendor
Business secrets, unreleased financials, M&A data
Personal data subject to GDPR, CCPA, HIPAA, etc.
SCCs or adequacy decision; audit logging; BAA for PHI

Claude's Enterprise API supports a zero-data-retention mode (prompt and response not stored by Anthropic) that moves some Confidential workloads into the approved zone. Verify the exact data handling guarantees in your current Anthropic contract before updating catalog entries to allow Confidential data.
Defining Approved and Prohibited Use Cases

Listing approved use cases per model is as important as listing prohibited ones. Approval without scope creates ambiguity — a developer told only that 'Claude Sonnet is approved' may reasonably use it for legal document drafting or HR screening, which may not have been reviewed for the liability and discrimination risks those use cases carry.

# catalog-entry: claude-sonnet-4-6
approved_use_cases:
  - id: code-assist
    description: "Generate, review, and explain code; suggest refactors"
    data_classes: [public, internal]
    notes: "Do not include secrets, API keys, or customer PII in prompts"

  - id: doc-summarization
    description: "Summarize internal documents, meeting notes, reports"
    data_classes: [public, internal]
    notes: "Classify output before distributing — LLM may hallucinate"

  - id: classification
    description: "Classify support tickets, content moderation, intent routing"
    data_classes: [public, internal]
    notes: "Human review required for high-stakes decisions (e.g., account suspension)"

prohibited_use_cases:
  - id: legal-advice
    reason: "Unauthorized practice of law; significant liability risk"

  - id: medical-diagnosis
    reason: "Not a licensed medical device; PHI not approved for this entry"

  - id: hr-screening
    reason: "Discrimination risk under EEOC; requires separate legal and HR review"

  - id: confidential-data
    reason: "Confidential classification not approved — see restricted-entry catalog"
    alternative: "Submit a Restricted Model Request form for per-project approval

Store catalog entries in version control alongside your infrastructure-as-code. This creates an audit trail of when use cases were added or removed, and by whom — important for demonstrating governance maturity to external auditors.
Access Control: Restricting API Key Issuance

The catalog defines what is allowed; the access control layer enforces it. The mechanism for enforcement is API key governance — who can obtain a key, which key is scoped to which catalog entry, and how key usage is monitored.

One API key per engineering team, scoped to a set of approved use cases. The key is owned by a service account, rotated on a schedule, and stored in the organization's secrets manager (e.g., AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault).
For use cases with tighter data-classification constraints, issue a dedicated key per project. This limits blast radius if a key is compromised and makes per-project spend tracking straightforward.
Separate, rate-limited keys for local development and experimentation. Sandbox keys must never touch production data. Revoke automatically after 90 days of inactivity.

# Example: self-service key issuance via internal CLI backed by the catalog
# Developer runs this from their laptop; the CLI checks catalog approval automatically

$ ai-keys request \
    --model claude-sonnet-4-6 \
    --use-case code-assist \
    --project my-code-review-tool \
    --data-class internal

# CLI behavior:
# 1. Validates that claude-sonnet-4-6 + code-assist + internal is in the catalog
# 2. Checks if requesting team already has a key covering this scope
# 3. If approved: creates a key in the vendor console, stores in Vault, emails team lead
# 4. If not in catalog: opens a Model Request ticket for the governance review flow
# 5. Sets key expiry to 365 days; enforces rotation reminder at 330 days

Key created: sk-ant-... (stored in Vault at secret/ai-keys/my-code-review-tool)
Expires: 2026-07-01
Usage dashboard: https://internal-platform/ai-keys/my-code-review-tool

This self-service model — with automated catalog validation — eliminates the approval bottleneck while maintaining governance. Developers get keys in minutes when the use case is already in the catalog; novel use cases go through the governance review flow.

A catalog that is not actively maintained becomes a liability: entries pointing at expired contracts, models that have been deprecated by the vendor, or use cases that no longer reflect current legal requirements. Build a review cadence into the catalog governance process from day one.

Check for new Claude model releases and deprecation notices from Anthropic. Update the catalog to add new models (after fast-track security review if they are in the same contract tier) and flag deprecated model IDs for migration.

Review pricing changes and compare against current commit utilization. Assess whether any team has been using models outside their approved catalog scope — a signal that the catalog is either too restrictive or not being enforced.

Full audit of all catalog entries: re-validate data classification approvals against current legal standards, confirm DPA and BAA status, verify contract expiry dates, and review prohibited use case list against regulatory changes.

Trigger an unscheduled review when Anthropic publishes a security advisory, when a significant regulatory change affects AI data handling (e.g., new GDPR guidance), or when the organization's own data classification policy is updated.

Run this audit as part of your monthly review cycle. The output feeds a migration backlog: for each deprecated model, identify which teams are using it (via API key → project mapping) and schedule a coordinated upgrade.
Enforcing Catalog Policy at the Code Layer

Catalog governance is only as strong as its enforcement. A policy document that developers can bypass by requesting API keys directly from the vendor console provides the illusion of governance without the substance. The following patterns enforce catalog policy programmatically — making compliant behavior the path of least resistance.

Rather than giving each team direct access to Anthropic's API, route all traffic through an internal API gateway that validates requests against the catalog before forwarding them. This pattern adds a minimal latency overhead (~1–5ms) while enabling centralized enforcement, monitoring, and key management.

The gateway holds the Anthropic API key — individual teams never see it. Teams authenticate to the gateway using their own service account credentials (OAuth2, mTLS, or an internal token service). This decouples key rotation from application deployments and centralizes audit logging.

Add a catalog compliance check to your CI/CD pipeline that fails the build if a code change introduces a model ID not present in the catalog. This prevents catalog violations from reaching production even in organizations where the API gateway is not yet deployed.

Add this script to your pre-commit hooks and CI pipeline. A developer who hard-codes a model ID not in the catalog gets an immediate, actionable error message rather than discovering the violation during a security audit.

Claude model identifiers include a version date (e.g., claude-haiku-4-5-20251001). Anthropic periodically releases new versions of each model family and deprecates older ones. Your catalog policy must define how the organization handles model version transitions.
Manual migration required when version deprecated
Always on newest capabilities; no migration overhead
Behavior may change between calls; harder to test
Centrally controlled; teams see stable ID
Requires gateway infrastructure; adds ops complexity

The recommended enterprise approach is pinned version IDs for production workloads, with a catalog-managed migration policy: when Anthropic announces a deprecation, the catalog team publishes a migration guide, teams have 60 days to upgrade, and the catalog entry for the deprecated model is marked 'sunset pending' to block new key issuance while existing workloads migrate.
Anthropic announces deprecation of a model version (e.g., in release notes or via account team email).
Catalog team adds a 'sunset_date' field to the affected catalog entry and sets 'new_key_issuance' to false.
Automated notification sent to all API key owners for the deprecated model — include the sunset date and the approved replacement model ID.
Teams update their model ID in code and run evaluation suite against the replacement model to confirm parity.
After 60 days (or sooner if teams complete migration), the catalog entry is marked 'deprecated' and the API gateway begins returning HTTP 410 Gone for requests to the deprecated model ID.
Verify no remaining traffic to the deprecated model via usage analytics; archive the catalog entry.
Catalog Policy Exceptions and Fast-Track Approval

No catalog design survives first contact with production without an exception process. Teams will encounter legitimate needs for a model, data classification, or use case not currently in the catalog. The exception process must be fast enough to not become a bottleneck for product development, while still providing meaningful governance.

Reserve the full governance review (security + legal + catalog committee) for high-risk additions — new vendors, Restricted data classifications, or novel use case categories. Define a fast-track path for low-risk catalog extensions: new Claude models in the same tier, new use cases that are substantively similar to approved ones, or minor data classification boundary changes within the same vendor contract.
New Claude model, same contract tier
Existing model, new use case (similar to approved)
Existing model, Internal → Confidential data class

# catalog-exception-request.yaml
request_id: CATALOG-EX-2024-0089
submitted_by: alice@example.com
submitted_date: 2024-11-15
team: payments-ml

model_id: claude-opus-4-8
use_case: contract-extraction
data_class: confidential

business_justification: >
  The legal team processes 500+ vendor contracts per quarter. Manual extraction
  of payment terms takes 2 hours per contract. Claude Opus extracts key clauses
  in under 30 seconds with human review of output before action. No contract
  content is stored by Anthropic under our zero-data-retention addendum.

security_controls:
  - Zero data retention API mode confirmed active (addendum signed 2024-08-01)
  - Prompts stripped of counterparty names before submission (anonymization layer)
  - Human review gate before any extracted clause is acted upon

approval_status: fast_track_eligible
reviewer: security-team
review_sla: 2024-11-19

Store exception requests in the same version control repository as the catalog. A merged PR that adds a new catalog entry, referencing the approved exception request, creates a complete audit trail: who requested it, what justification was given, who approved it, and when it became active.
Communicating the Catalog to Engineering Teams

The best-designed catalog fails if engineers don't know it exists or find it too cumbersome to use. Catalog communication is a product design problem: the catalog must be discoverable, queryable, and actionable from a developer's natural workflow.

Publish the catalog as a searchable page in your internal portal (Backstage, Confluence, Notion, or similar). Surface the 'approved use cases' and 'prohibited use cases' prominently — these answer the question developers actually ask: 'Can I use Claude for X?'
Build a simple Slack command (/ai-catalog claude-sonnet-4-6) that returns the catalog entry for a model. Developers get instant answers without leaving their communication tool.

Provide a standard README section for AI-integrated services: model ID, catalog entry link, data classification, approved use cases. Make this part of the new-service template so every AI-using service self-documents its catalog compliance.

Include the model catalog in the engineering onboarding checklist. A 10-minute orientation that covers 'what models are approved, how to request a key, and what data you may send' prevents a large class of governance violations before they happen.

An approved model catalog is the foundation of enterprise AI governance. It maps each Claude model to the data classifications and use cases it is approved for, creates an audit trail of who reviewed each entry and when, and provides the enforcement surface for API key issuance. The key operational discipline is maintenance: update the catalog on new model releases, reassess data classification approvals when contract or regulatory conditions change, and enforce the catalog through automated key issuance workflows rather than manual approval queues.
